Facts.text: Battle of Adrianople: Bulgarians and Cumans under Tsar Kaloyan of Bulgaria ambush and defeat the Latin Emperor of Constantinople, Baldwin I
Kublai is acclaimed the Great Khan by a Mongol Great Council
Battle of Formigny: French defeat the English decisively in the Hundred Years' War, paving the way for the capture of English strongholds in Normandy
Nicolás de Ovando y Cáceres, new Governor of the Indies, lands in Hispaniola, replacingChristopher Columbus
Pánfilo the Narváez, Spanish conquistador arrives in Florida with 350 men to a hostile reception from native indians
Flemish painter Pieter Stevens is appointed as the royal painter of Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II in Prague
Battle of Rain; Swedes under Gustavus Adolphus defeat Count Tilly of the Holy Roman Empire during the Thirty Years' War.
The Commonwealth of England and the United Netherlands sign a peace treaty ending the First Anglo-Dutch War
Pocotaligo Massacre of four South Carolina representatives triggers the start of the Yamasee War in colonial South Carolina
Great Northern War: Prussian troops occupy Swedish controlled German port of Wismar
Duchess of Kingston is found guilty of bigamy
American Revolution: the Continental Congress ratified preliminary articles of peace, ending the seven-year-long war with Great Britain.
Britain, Netherlands & Prussia sign peace treaty
Bank of England issues first £5 note

The American Asylum, now known as the American School for the Deaf (ASD), is the first permanent US school for the deaf, founded by Rev. Thomas Gallaudet, Dr. Mason Cogswell, and teacher Laurent Clerc in West Hartford, Connecticut
City of San Francisco incorporated
Earl G Andressy sentenced to death in Hungary
Dutch Protestant church petition King William III against re-establishment of Roman Catholic bishops in the Netherlands, he does so anyway
Battle of Azimghur, Mexicans defeat Spanish loyalists
General Steeles' Union troops occupies Camden, Arkansas
Mobile, Alabama is captured by Union forces after a siege led by General Canby
Last day US silver coins allow to circulate in Canada
NY legislature passes compulsory education law
World's first home telephone is installed in Somerville, Massachusetts at the house of Charles Williams Jr.
Harley Procter introduces Ivory Soap
Josephine Blatt (US) makes hip-and-harness lift of 3564 lb (record)
1st Modern Summer Olympic Games close at Panathenaic Stadium in Athens, Greece; USA wins gold medal count with 11 and Greece the total medal count with 46
An early 50 mile race is won by an electric car in over 2 hrs
Exposition Universelle (World's Fair) in Paris opens (till 12th Nov)
1st British motorized burial
PopeLeo XIIIencyclical "On Church in US"
Rioting and arson continue in Russia with peasants plundering estates to find food.
Russian minister of interior and head of secret police, Sipyengin, is assassinated by the 'Terror Brigade' of the Socialist Revolutionaries
The Armenian organization AGBU is established.
Jack Lawrence Theater (Playhouse) opens at 137 W 48th St NYC
Manuel de Falla's ballet "El Amor Brujo" premieres in Madrid
New Canadian small cent coin is released
Sacco & Vanzetti Trial: paymaster shot and killed along with his guard

Black Friday in Britain: leaders of transport and rail unions announce a decision not to call for strike action in support of the miners; despite widespread feeling decision a breach of solidarity and a betrayal of the miners
The legendary Poodle Dog Restaurant closes in San Francisco
1st sound on film public performance shown at Rialto Theater (NYC)
Insulin becomes generally available for diabetics
Flemish-Walloon riots in Louvain, Belgium, 1 dead
Rand McNally publishes its first road atlas.
WHO-AM in Des Moines Iowa begins radio transmissions
NHL's NY Americans (formerly Hamilton Tigers) 1st game, lose 3-1

Sesquicentennial Stadium opens in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; renamed Philadelphia Municipal Stadium, later renamed JFK Stadium (demolished 1992); played host to Tunney-Demspy fight (1926); 41 Army-Navy football games; professional football; and many concerts, including the US portion of Live-Aid (1985)
Switzerland & USSR agree to diplomatic relations
Alioto's on Fisherman's Wharf (San Francisco) forms
1st backwards walk across American begins
Irish government authorizes establishment of Aer Lingus (Aerloingeas) as the national airline of the Republic of Ireland
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Detroit Red Wings beat New York Rangers, 3-0 for a 3-2 series win; back-to-back titles for Red Wings
Anti-Jewish riots break out in  in Dąbrowa Tarnowska, Poland
Albert Lebrun elected president of France
British troops land at Narvik, Norway
1st helicopter flight of 1 hr duration, Stratford, Ct
Metropolitan Life Insurances issues a $225 million check to Chase
British Army liberates Nazi concentration camp Bergen-Belsen
Operations begin at Radio Netherlands World radio
1st Jewish-Arab military battle, arabs defeated
Indian territory of Himachal Pradesh created
KCPX (now KTVX) TV channel 4 in Salt Lake City, UT (ABC) 1st broadcast
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Redemptoris nostri
Michael Gorsira is 1st person in charge of Curacao
Archaeologist Albert Ruz discovers the tomb of Pakal, the greatest ruler of the Maya city of Palenque
Franklin National Bank issues 1st bank credit card
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Detroit Red Wings beat Montreal Canadiens, 3-0 for a 4-0 series sweep
The maiden flight of the US Air Force B-52 Stratofortress prototype, designed and built by Boeing
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Malans National Party wins South African elections
WHP TV channel 21 in Harrisburg, PA (CBS) begins broadcasting
KARK TV channel 4 in Little Rock, AR (NBC) begins broadcasting
Orioles 1st game in Baltimore beat White Sox 3-1
WHO TV channel 13 in Des Moines, IA (NBC) begins broadcasting
Yankees dedicate a plaque to Edward Barrow
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
KTVI TV channel 2 in Saint Louis, MO (ABC) begins broadcasting
Saturday mail delivery restored in the US after Congress gives the Post Office $41 million
1st baseball game in California, SF Giants beat LA Dodgers, 8-0
Guy Carawan sings "We Shall Overcome" to the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee in Raleigh, popularizing the song as a protest anthem
Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), organizes at Shaw U
US national debt above $300,000,000,000
Chesapeake Bay Bridge opens (world's longest)
NFL changes penalty flag from white to bright gold
KHET TV channel 11 in Honolulu, HI (PBS) begins broadcasting
North Korea shoots at US airplane above Japanese sea
Libyan leader Gadaffi launches "Green Revolution"
WMGZ TV channel 16 in Mayaguez, PR ([M]) begins broadcasting
WPSJ TV channel 14 in Ponce, PR ([P]) begins broadcasting
A member of the British Army is shot dead by the Official IRA in the Divis area of Belfast.
A member of the Official Irish Republican Army is shot dead by British soldiers at Joy Street in the Markets area of Belfast close to his home
President Diori Hamani is deposed in a military coup in Niger
Gabon amends constitution
Yankee stadium reopens, Yanks beat Twins after trailing 4-0
1st baseball game at Montreal's Olympic Stadium
43 die as 2 express trains collide head-on south of Bologna, Italy
Great Britain performs nuclear test
Magnitude 6.9 earthquake strikes the Montenegro and Albanian coast, killing 136 people
Janet Cooke says her Pulitzer award-winning story about an 8-year-old heroin addict is a lie, Washington Post relinquishes Pulitzer Prize on fabricated story
Apollo Computer announces the DN400, DN420, and landscape display
Tokyo Disneyland opens
Extremist Sikhs plunder 40 stations in Punjab India
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Challenger moves to launch pad for 51-B missing
US air raids Libya, responding to La Belle disco, Berlin bombing
US launches Operation El Dorado Canyon against Libya
Alfred Uhry's "Driving Miss Daisy" premieres in NYC
Meteorite explode above Indonesia
Hillsborough disaster: 96 people are crushed to death and 766 are injured at Hillsborough Football Stadium in Sheffield, England, during an FA Cup semi-final between Liverpool and Nottingham Forest
Students in Beijing pro-democracy protests
Sue Marchiano wins 3rd World Cup female marathon (2:30:48)
Then largest lottery in North America ($69M) drawn in Illinois
Greenidge & Haynes make 298 opening stand (v England), combination best
Sketch comedy TV series "In Living Color" premieres on FOX TV
Dutch checker Ton Sijbrands improves world record blind checker games (15 wins)
East-Europe Bank forms in London
Europe foreign ministers lift most remaining sanctions against South Africa
Former child actor Adam Rich (Eight Is Enough) charged with burglary
New York State raises maximum unemployment benefits to $280 per week
Sacramento Kings set NBA record, losing 35th consecutive game on road
Billionaire Leona Helmsley is sent to jail for tax evasion
Lincoln Hospital in Bronx. New York loses its accreditation
National Assembly of Vietnam adopts the Constitution of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam
Indians loses 1st game at Jacobs Field, KC wins 2-1
WMMS-FM's Jeff & Flash, & entire station staff, are fired
America Online (AOL) begins service in Japan
Baseball honorsJackie Robinsonby retiring #42 for all teams
Fire sweeps through a campsite of Muslims making the Hajj pilgrimage in Mecca, killing 217 to 300 people and injuring 1,300
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Charlotte, North Carolina on WXRC 95.7 FM
NFL Draft: Penn State defensive end Courtney Brown first pick by Cleveland Browns
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, GC at Desert Mountain: Doug Tewell wins his second & final Champions Tour major by 9 strokes from Mike McCullough
Air China Boeing 767-200, flight CA129, crashes into a hillside during heavy rain and fog near Busan, South Korea, killing 128 people

Even though official government sources state that Argentina's GDP will actually grow this year, private consulting firms state that Argentine economy has actually been in recession since October 2008
Volcanic ash from the eruption of Eyjafjallajökull in Iceland leads to the closure of airspace over most of Europe
"Black Friday" for online poker in the US: indictment United States v. Scheinberg shuts down sites, accusing companies of fraud and money laundering
400 Islamist Militants escape from a Pakistan prison after an insurgent attack
China loosens monetary policy and allows the Yuan to fluctuate up to 1% against the US dollar
US Secret Service inappropriate conduct scandal begins with at least 11 people implicated
33 people are killed and 163 are injured in a wave of bombings across Iraq
Suicide car bomb targets buses carrying Syrian evacuees at Rashidin, 126 killed including 70 children
126.2 cm (49.69") of rainfall in a day, Waipā Garden (Kauai). Hawaii (state record, breaking previous record of 1956)
3 days after winning Hobey Baker Award as top college hockey player, Cale Makar becomes first defenseman to score in NHL debut during the playoffs in Colorado's 6-2 Game 3 win over Calgary
Los Angeles Clippers overcome an NBA record 31-point deficit to score an improbable 135-131 Game 2 playoff victory over the Golden State Warriors in Oakland; shooting guard Lou Williams 36 points
Measles cases jump 300% in first three months of 2019, according to World Health Organization, largest rise in Africa (700%) with 800 deaths in Madagascar
Revised death count for New York taking into account assumed COVID-19 deaths makes the city's per-capita death rate higher than Italy at 10,367 at this date
US's deadliest day during COVID-19 pandemic with 2,752 deaths reported

"The failed response in Brazil has caused a humanitarian catastrophe" reports Dr. Christos Christou, president of Doctors Without Borders, as country records a quarter of world's COVID-19 deaths in last week
A shooting at a FedEx facility in Indianapolis, Indiana, leaves eight dead and five injured
Court in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire, sentences former warlord Amadé Ouérémi to a life sentence for massacres by his militia after the 2010 election
India record over 200,000 (200,739) daily new cases of COVID-19 for the first time with 1,038 deaths amid massive second wave
Germany ends its use of nuclear power, closing its last three nuclear power plants - Emsland, Isar 2 and Neckarwestheim to focus on renewable energy
Indian Atiq Ahmed, former lawmaker and convicted criminal, assassinated live on TV along with his brother Ashraf in Prayagraj, Uttar Pradesh, while under police guard
AI systems now so advanced they nearly match or exceed humans in reading comprehension, image classification and competition-level mathematics, according to major new report
Former political staffer Bruce Lehrmann loses his high profile defamation case against an Australian TV network, the court finds he did rape Brittany Higgins in 2019 at parliament
Sudan is on the edge of collapse warn aid agencies, on the one-year anniversary of a conflict that has displaced over 8.6 million people, including 2 million who fled to neighbouring countries
148 passengers die after a motorized wooden boat catches fire and capsizes in the Congo River near the town of Mbandaka
Sandstorm puts at least 3,700 people in hospitals with breathing difficulties, and two airports suspend flights due to poor visibility in central and southern Iraq
PopeInnocent IVrefuses Jews of Cordova, Spain their request to build a synagogue
At war with Castile,John I of Portugalinstructs his ambassadors to negotiate an alliance withRichard IIof England and to raise loans to pay his troops

Christopher Columbusis received by the Spanish monarchsIsabella IandFerdinand IIin Barcelona upon his return from the New World. Columbus presents kidnapped Taínos indigenous people, plants and items collected from the Caribbean.
Thomas Cromwellis appointed Chief Secretary to KingHenry VIIIof England
Portuguese assembly the Cortes of Tomar recognizesPhilip II of Spainas King of Portugal after a succession crisis
Hugo Grotiusarrives in France after escaping prison in a book chest
Battle of Dharmat won by future Moghul emperorAurangzeb, who defeats his brother and designated heir Dara Shikoh
French KingLouis XIVdeclares war on Spain
Charles XIIsucceeds his father, Charles XI, as King of Sweden
Johann Sebastian Bach's "St Matthew Passion" premieres at the Thomaskirche in Leipzig, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany)
"Serse", an Italian opera byGeorge Frideric Handelpremieres at the King's Theatre in Haymarket, London
Samuel Johnson's "A Dictionary of the English Language" is published in London
William Wordsworthand his sister Dorothy see a "long belt" of daffodils, inspiring the former to pen "I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud".
Federal army of 75,000 volunteers is mobilized by PresidentAbraham Lincolnat the start of the American Civil War
American poetEmily Dickinsonfirst corresponds with author and future literary mentor Thomas Wentworth Higginson, a relationship that lasts the rest of her life
Otto von Bismarckelevated to rank of Count of Bismarck-Schönhausen
First Impressionist art exhibition opens in Paris, featuresClaude Monet,Edgar Degas,Pierre-Auguste Renoir,Camille PissarroandBerthe Morisot
Guy de Maupassant's short story masterpiece "Boule de Suif" (Dumpling) is first published in the collection "Les Soirées de Médan"
General Electric Company formed by merger ofThomas Edison's General Electric Company with Thomson-Houston Electric Company, arranged byJ. P. Morganand incorporated in NY
PopeLeo XIIIissues an allocution deploring hostile actions against the Roman Catholic Church throughout Europe
William Howard Taftis first US President to throw out the 1st ball at a baseball game
Walter Johnsonpitches a record tying 4 strike outs in an inning
NY GiantRube Marquardno-hits Bkln, 2-0
The Sixtus Affair: French MinisterGeorges Clémenceaupublishes secret documents showing Austrian Emperor Karl made overtures of peace to France
Yankees sluggerBabe Ruthhits #1 of MLB record season 60 HRs; tees off on A's Howard Ehmke in 1st inning of New York's 6-3 win over Philadelphia
George VIawards George Cross to people of Malta
34th US PresidentFDRburied in grounds of his Hyde Park home, New York state
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical "Communium Interpretes Dolorum"
Jackie Robinsonbecomes the first African American to play in Major League Baseball for the Brooklyn Dodgers
Ray Krocopens the first McDonald's Inc. fast food restaurant in Des Plaines, Illinois
10th Emmy Awards: Gunsmoke, Robert Young &Jane Wyattwin
Fidel Castrobegins US goodwill tour
US Secretary of StateJohn Foster Dullesresigns
The Rolling Stonesrelease "Aftermath", their fourth studio album in UK (6th in US)
Houston Astros beat NY Mets, 1-0, in 24 innings at the Astrodome; starting pitchersTom Seaverand Don Wilson pitch 10 and 9 innings respectively in game that lasts over 6 hours

Hi Records releases "Green Is Blues", the second studio album by American soul singer-songwriterAl Green; his first pairing with producer Willie Mitchell led to a long-time collaboration and string of hits in the early 1970s
43rd Academy Awards: "Patton", George C Scott &Glenda Jacksonwin
Barbra Streisand,James Taylor,Carole King, andQuincy Jonesperform benefit concert forGeorge McGovernfor President campaign at the Forum in Inglewood, California
Wendy Wasserstein's play "Heidi Chronicles" premieres in NYC
Magic Johnsonsets NBA record for career assists with 9,898
Jay Leno's final appearance as permanent guest host of The Tonight Show
NY Islander coachAl Arboursets a record for coaching the most NHL games at 1,438
William Shatner,Leonard Nimoyand DeForest Kelley inducted into National Association of Broadcasters Hall of Fame

Jazz singerTony Bennettrecords a session for "MTV's Unplugged" series at Sony Studios, NYC, featuring the Ralph Sharon trio, and guest appearances byElvis Costello, and k.d.lang; album release wins 2 Grammy Awards
Disaster film "Volcano" directed by Mick Jackson, and starringTommy Lee JonesandAnne Hechepremieres in South Korea
Giant Sequoia National Monument proclamation signed by PresidentBill Clintonin California, preserving one-third of all giant sequoia groves, the world's largest tree
Hologram of late rapper Tupac features on stage withSnoop DoggandDr. Dreat Coachella music festival
TV comedy drama "Girls" created by and starringLena Dunhampremieres on HBO
Nicolás Madurois narrowly elected President of Venezuela
US TV series "Fargo" created by Noah Hawley, starringBilly Bob Thornton, Allison Tolman, Colin Hanks, andMartin Freemanpremieres on FX
Laureus World Sports Awards, Shanghai Grand Theatre, Shanghai, China: Sportsman:Novak Đoković; Sportswoman: Genzebe Dibaba; Team: German Men's National Football team
Aretha Franklinposthumously receives the Pulitzer Prize Special Citation honor, first individual woman to win it since 1930
South Korea is 1st country to hold a general election under COVID-19, PresidentMoon Jae-in's ruling Democratic party wins in a landslide
Coachella Valley music festival returns after a two-year hiatus withHarry Stylesthe headlining act on opening night in front of 100,000
Donald Trumpbecomes the first former US President to stand trial on criminal charges as his hush money case begins in New York
Abraham Lincolndies nine hours after he is shot by John Wilkes Booth while attending the play "Our American Cousin" at Ford's Theatre in Washington
RMS Titanicsinks at 2:20 AM off Newfoundland as the band plays on, with the loss of between 1,490 and 1,635 lives
Paris cathedral Notre Dame catches fire, toppling its spire and destroying its roof
Mimar Sinan, Ottoman architect and civil engineer for Sultans Suleiman the Magnificent, Selim II, and Murad III, born in Ağırnas, Karaman Eyalet, Ottoman Empire (d. 1588)
Pietro Cataldi, Italian mathematician, born in Bologna, Papal States (now Italy) (d. 1626)
Claudius Salmasius [Claude Saumaise], French linguist, born in Semur-en-Auxois, France (d. 1653)
Valentin Molitor, Swiss composer, born in Rapperswil, Switzerland (d. 1713)
Robert Sibbald, Scottish physician, born in Edinburgh (d. 1722)
Christian V, King of Denmark and Norway, born in Duborg Castle, Flensburg (d. 1699)
Pierre Poiret, French mystic, born in Metz, France (d. 1719)
Domenico Gabrielli, Italian Baroque composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1690)
Jann van Huysum, Dutch painter (still lives of flowers and fruit), born in Amsterdam (d. 1749)
Catherine I, Empress of Russia (1725-27), born in Višķi, Daugavpils Municipality (d. 1727)
Johann Friedrich Fasch, German violinist, harpsichordist, organist, kapellmeister, and composer, born in Buttelstedt, Duchy of Saxe-Weimar (d. 1758)
Ferdinand Zellbell the elder, Swedish composer, born in Uppsala, Sweden (d. 1765)
Marie-Anne de Cupis de Camargo, French dancer, born in Brussels, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1770)
William Cullen, Scottish physician and chemist, born in Hamilton, Lanarkshire, Scotland (d. 1790)
Johann Heinrich Zang, German composer, cantor, and organist (A student of J.S. Bach), born in Zella St Blasii, Duchy of Saxe-Gotha-Altenburg (d. 1811)
Charles Willson Peale, American painter remembered for portraits of leading American Revolution figures (George Washington), born in Chester, Province of Maryland (d. 1827)
Friedrich Bouterwek, German philosopher and critic, born in Oker, Hanover, Germany (d. 1828)
Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, French naturalist, born in Étampes, France (d. 1844)
Walter Channing, American physician who was the first to use anesthesia during childbirth, born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1876)
Friedrich Georg Wilhelm von Struve, German-Russian astronomer, born in Altona, Duchy of Holstein, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany) (d. 1864)
Jean Pierre Flourens, French physiologist, born in Maureilhan, France (d. 1867)
James Clark Ross, British Royal Navy explorer (British Antarctic), born in London (d. 1862)
Hermann Grassmann, German linguist and mathematician, born in Stettin, Province of Pomerania, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1877)
Théodore Rousseau, French painter, born in Paris (d. 1867)
John Lothrop Motley, American historian and author (The Rise of the Dutch Republic), born in Dorchester, Boston (d. 1877)
Evander McNair, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Laurel Hill, North Carolina (d. 1902)
Joseph E. Brown, American attorney and politician (42nd Governor of Georgia), born in Pickens, South Carolina (d. 1894)
Napoleon Jackson Tecumseh Dana, American Major General (Union Army), born in Eastport, Maine (d. 1905)
Julius Tausch, German composer, born in Dessau, Duchy of Anhalt, German Confederation (d. 1895)
Mary Harris Thompson, 1st American woman surgeon, born in Fort Ann, New York (d. 1895)
Wilhelm Busch, German poet, born in Wiedensahl, Kingdom of Hanover (d. 1908)
Horace Porter, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Huntingdon, Pennsylvania (d. 1921)
Joseph E. Seagram, Canadian distillery founder (Seagram Distilleries), born in Fisher's Mills, Canada West (d. 1919)
Carl Eilhardt, German composer and conductor, born in Erfurt, Province of Saxony, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1911)
Henry James, American-British author (Turn of the Screw, Bostonians), born in New York City (d. 1916)
Dave Gregory, Australian cricket batsman (Australia's 1st Test captain; 3 Tests; NSW CA), born in Fairy Meadow, Australia (d. 1919)
John Munro Longyear, American capitalist and developer of timber and mineral lands, born in Lansing, Michigan (d. 1922)
Bliss Carman, Canadian poet, born in Fredericton, New Brunswick (d. 1929)
Juliette Atkinson, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1895, 97-98), born in Rahway, New Jersey (d. 1944)
George Harrison Shull, American plant geneticist, born in Clark County, Ohio (d. 1954)
Johannes Stark, German physicist (Stark Effect; Nobel Prize in Physics -1919), and Nazi sympathizer, born in Schickenhof, German Empire (d. 1957)
Klaziena "Ina" Boudier-Bakker, Dutch playwright and novelist (Poverty), born in Amsterdam (d. 1966)
Robert Walser, Swiss writer, born in Biel (d. 1956)
Melville Henry Cane, American lawyer and poet (Making a Poem), born in Plattsburgh, New York (d. 1980)
Giovanni Amendola, Italian anti-fascist and editor-in-chief (Il Mondo), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1926)
Stanley Bruce, 8th Prime Minister of Australia, born in St Kilda, Victoria, Australia (d. 1967)
Tadeusz Kutrzeba, Polish general during the Second Polish Republic, born in Kraków, Galicia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1947)
Nikolay Gumilyov, Russian poet, born in Kronshtadt, Russia (d. 1921)
Mike Brady, American golfer (US Open 1911, 19 runner-up; 9 PGA Tour titles), born in Brighton, Massachusetts (d. 1972)
Florence Bates, American actress (Moon & Sixpence, Love Crazy, San Antonio), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 1954)
Maximilian Kronberger, German poet, born in Berlin (d. 1904)
A. Philip Randolph, American labor leader (Railroad Porter's Union), born in Crescent City, Florida (d. 1979)
Thomas Hart Benton, American painter and muralist (Lonesome Road), born in Neosho, Missouri (d. 1975)
Väinö Raitio, Finnish composer, born in Sortavala, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1945)
Wallace Reid, American actor, director and screenwriter referred to as "the screen's most perfect lover" (Every Inch a Man), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1923)
Corrie ten Boom, Dutch author (The Hiding Place) and Holocaust survivor, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Theo Osterkamp, World War I and World War II German fighter pilot, born in Düren, Rhine Province, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1975)
Bessie Smith, American blues singer known as the "Empress of the Blues" (recorded over 200 songs), born in Chattanooga, Tennessee (d. 1937)
Clark McConachy, New Zealand billiards player (World Professional C'ships 1951), born in Glenorchy, New Zealand (d. 1980)
Corrado Alvaro, Italian writer (Gente in Aspromonte), born in San Luca, Italy (d. 1956)
Nikolay Nikolayevich Semyonov, Russian chemist and Nobel Prize winner known for his work on chain reactions, born in Saratov, Russia (d. 1986)
Pedro Regas, Greek stage actor (Only Angels Have Wings, Pat Paulsen's Half a Comedy Hour), born in Goranoi, Greece (d. 1974)
Harry F. V. Edward, British 100m/200m runner (Olympic bronze 1920), born in Berlin (d. 1973)
Marian Jordan, American actress, radio comedienne (Molly-Fibber McGee & Molly), born in Peoria, Illinois (d. 1961)
Nini de Boël, Flemish operetta singer (White Horse), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1982)
Joe Davis, English snooker player (World Champion 1927-40, 1946), born in Whitwell, Derbyshire (d. 1978)
Fernando Pessa, Portuguese journalist, born in Aveiro District, Portugal (d. 2002)
Erich Arendt, German poet and writer, born in Neuruppin, Germany (d. 1984)
John Williams, British actor (Family Affair, Dial M for Murder), born in Chalfont St. Giles, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1983)
Arshile Gorky, Armenian-American abstract painter, born in Khorgom, Vilayet of Van, Ottoman Empire (d. 1948)
Sandy Bell, South African cricket fast bowler (16 Tests, 48 wickets, BB 6/99; Western Province, Rhodesia), born in East London, South Africa (d. 1985)
Gerald Abrahams, English author (Teach Yourself Chess), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1980)
Nikolaas Tinbergen, Dutch-British biologist and zoologist (Nobel 1973), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1988)
Eden Ahbez [George Aberle], American songwriter ("Nature Boy"; "Lonely Island"), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1995)
Ralph Grey, Baron Grey of Naunton, Governor of Northern Ireland (1968-73), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1999)
Jacob Fresco, Dutch architect (Antilles Brewery/Autonomy Monument), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1990)
John Gregory, British ballet dancer (Harlequin Ballet Company, 1959-69), and writer, born in Norwich, England (d. 1996)[1]
Elizabeth Catlett, African-American sculptor and print maker (Mother and Child), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 2012)
Alfred S. Bloomingdale, American businessman and heir to the Bloomingdale's department store, born in New York City (d. 1982)
Hans Conried, American actor (Peter Pan; Bullwinkle Show; Make Room for Daddy - "Uncle Tonoose"; The 5,000 Fingers of Dr. T), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1982)
James Kee, American politician (US Representatives for West Virginia, 1965-73), born in Bluefield, West Virginia (d. 1989)
Pietro Grossi, Italian composer, and electronic music pioneer, born in Venice, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2002)
John Baragrey, American actor (Creeper, The Loves of Carmen), born in Haleyville, Alabama (d. 1975)
Clive Beadon, British RAF Wing Commander and pilot (WWII), born in Conoor near Poona, India (d. 1996)
Meriol Trevor, English novelist and biographer (Civil Prisoners), born in London (d. 2000)
Richard von Weizsacker, German politician and 1st president of reunited Germany (1990-94), born in Stuttgart, Germany (d. 2015)
Thomas Szasz, Hungarian-American psychiatrist (The Myth of Mental Illness, The Manufacture of Madness), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2012)
Georgy Beregovoy, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 3), born in Fedorivka, Poltava Oblast, Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic (d. 1995)
Hasrat Jaipuri [Iqbal Hussain], Indian poet and lyricist for Bollywood films, born in Jaipur, British India (d. 1999)
Michael Ansara, American actor (Broken Arrow, Centennial), born in Lowell, Massachusetts (d. 2013)
Douglas Wass, British civil servant (Permanent Secretary to HM Treasury, 1974-83), born in Wallasey, Merseyside, England (d. 2017)
Harvey Lembeck, American stage and screen actor (The Phil Silvers Show; Stalag 17; Beach Party films), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1982)
John Grigg, 2nd Baron Altrincham, British writer and historian, born in Westminster, London (d. 2001)
Neville Marriner, English violinist (London Symphony, 1954-69), and conductor (Academy of St Martin in the Fields, 1958-2011; Minnesota Orchestra, 1979-86), born in Lincoln (d. 2016)
Louis Stevens, American engineer (invented magnetic disk storage for IBM 305 RAMAC), born in Post, Texas (d. 2009)[1]
Jurriaan Schrofer, Dutch graphic designer, and sculptor, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1990)
Abha Gandhi, Indian servant and aid to Gandhi, born in India (d. 1995)
Annapurna Devi [Roshanara Khan], Indian classical surbahar (bass sitar) player, and music teacher, born in Maihar, British India (now part of Madhya Pradesh, India) (d. 2018)
Robert Mills, American physicist (quantum field theory), born in Englewood, New Jersey (d. 1999)
Donald Scott, Scottish rugby union outside back (10 Tests; South District, Edinburgh District), born in Langholm, Dumfries & Galloway, Scotland (d. 2024)
